description:
You are a tooling engineer who wants to create a **repeatable micro-benchmark harness** so that other developers can compare the wall-clock time and peak memory usage of three trivial reference commands.  
    Your job is to build everything needed and leave the results in a deterministic, machine-readable form that an automated test suite can parse.

    1. Create the workspace directory “/home/user/benchmarks”.
    2. Inside that directory write an executable Bash script named “run_benchmarks.sh”.  
       The script must do all of the following when a user runs it from the shell (e.g. “./run_benchmarks.sh”):
       • Run the three reference commands below **concurrently** (in parallel, not one after the other).  
         – Command A: `sleep 1`  
         – Command B: `sleep 2`  
         – Command C: `sleep 3`  
       • Measure each command’s wall-clock duration and maximum resident-set size (max RSS) using `/usr/bin/time -f "%e,%M"`.  
       • While the three commands are running, the script may not block sequentially; use background jobs, GNU parallel, or any other pure-user-space technique (do **not** require sudo).  
       • As soon as every job finishes, the script must assemble a single comma-separated value (CSV) file called “/home/user/benchmarks/benchmark_summary.csv” with **this exact layout**:  
         Line 1 (header) ‑ literally the text:  
             utility,elapsed_seconds,max_memory_kb  
         Line 2 ‑ data for Command A in this format (example numbers):  
             sleep_1s,1.01,1234  
         Line 3 ‑ data for Command B  
         Line 4 ‑ data for Command C  
         Notes on the CSV contents:  
           • Use the literal utility names “sleep_1s”, “sleep_2s”, “sleep_3s” (underscore, no spaces) for rows 2-4.  
           • `elapsed_seconds` comes directly from the first field (`%e`) produced by /usr/bin/time.  
           • `max_memory_kb` comes from the second field (`%M`).  
           • Exactly one comma (`,`) must separate the three fields; no extra whitespace is permitted.  
           • The benchmark_summary.csv file must end with a single trailing newline (`\n`).  
       • Additionally, the script must capture the raw /usr/bin/time output for each command, unmodified, into “/home/user/benchmarks/raw_benchmark.log”.  
         The log must contain one line per command in any order. Each line must be exactly:  
             <command_literal> <whitespace> <raw_time_output>  
         Example line (spacing not significant for verification):  
             sleep 1 1.00,1234  
         where “1.00,1234” is what /usr/bin/time prints for that invocation.

    3. Make sure “run_benchmarks.sh” has the executable bit set so that a plain user can run it (`chmod +x` style).

    4. After creating the script, **run it once** so that the two result files (“benchmark_summary.csv” and “raw_benchmark.log”) already exist in /home/user/benchmarks with fresh data.

    The automated grader will verify:

    • The directory “/home/user/benchmarks” exists.  
    • “run_benchmarks.sh” exists and is executable.  
    • “benchmark_summary.csv” exists, contains exactly 4 non-empty lines, follows the header and field constraints, and each elapsed_seconds value is  
            – ≥ its nominal sleep time (1, 2, or 3)  
            – < nominal sleep time + 0.5 seconds  
      (So 1.00–1.49 s is okay for sleep_1s, etc.).  
    • “raw_benchmark.log” exists and has three lines, each matching one of the commands (`sleep 1`, `sleep 2`, `sleep 3`) and containing the same numeric data that appears in the CSV row for that command.  
    • The script launches the sleeps in parallel: the total runtime of the script itself must be < 3.5 s even though the individual sleeps total 6 s when run sequentially.

    Summary of artefacts that **must** exist at the end:

    └─ /home/user/benchmarks  
       ├─ run_benchmarks.sh          (executable)  
       ├─ benchmark_summary.csv      (4-line CSV, exact format defined above)  
       └─ raw_benchmark.log          (3 lines, one per command)

    Do not hard-code absolute timing values; capture what /usr/bin/time reports at runtime.  
    Your task is finished when the two files contain valid data and all conditions above are met.
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth data used by the grader (not visible to the agent) -->
    <expected_directory>/home/user/benchmarks</expected_directory>

    <script_file>
        <path>/home/user/benchmarks/run_benchmarks.sh</path>
    </script_file>

    <summary_file>
        <path>/home/user/benchmarks/benchmark_summary.csv</path>
        <header_line>utility,elapsed_seconds,max_memory_kb</header_line>

        <!-- The grader will parse the CSV and then apply the numeric tolerances below -->
        <rows>
            <row utility="sleep_1s" min_elapsed="1.0" max_elapsed="1.5" />
            <row utility="sleep_2s" min_elapsed="2.0" max_elapsed="2.5" />
            <row utility="sleep_3s" min_elapsed="3.0" max_elapsed="3.5" />
        </rows>
    </summary_file>

    <raw_log_file>
        <path>/home/user/benchmarks/raw_benchmark.log</path>
        <!-- Each line must contain the literal command followed by the raw output
             from /usr/bin/time using the exact format "%e,%M". -->
    </raw_log_file>

    <!-- The grader will also measure how long the agent’s script takes to finish.
         It must be below 3.5 s, proving that the sleeps ran concurrently. -->
name: task_000000_abca1069
